title: Multimodal AI Systems
description: Build production applications that combine text, images, audio, and structured data using vision-language models, image embeddings, audio transcription, and multimodal RAG pipelines.
learningObjectives: Integrate vision-language models (VLMs) for image understanding
Build image search with CLIP-style embedding similarity
Implement audio transcription with Whisper and process audio pipelines
Design multimodal RAG that retrieves from text and image corpora
Handle multimodal input validation and cost management

sections.title: Why Multimodal Capabilities Are Now Production Requirements
sections.content: Enterprise AI use cases are overwhelmingly multimodal: invoice processing (image + text), technical documentation (diagrams + text), customer support (screenshots + conversation), quality control (images + specifications), meeting summarisation (audio + slides). Single-modality text AI can handle only a fraction of these. GPT-4o, Gemini 1.5, and Claude 3.5 Sonnet all support native multimodal input. Engineers who can build multimodal pipelines unlock an order-of-magnitude larger addressable problem space.

sections.title: Multimodal AI Architecture
sections.content:
**1. Vision-Language Models (VLMs)**
VLMs accept images as additional context alongside text. Architecture: image encoder (CLIP/SigLIP) maps image → embedding → concatenated with text tokens in the language model.
- Use cases: image description, visual QA, document OCR+understanding, chart interpretation, object detection
- API: pass base64-encoded image or URL in messages array alongside text

**2. CLIP-Style Image Embeddings**
CLIP (Contrastive Language-Image Pretraining) learns aligned image and text embeddings in the same space. Enables:
- Text-to-image search: embed query text → find similar image embeddings
- Image-to-image similarity: embed two images → cosine similarity
- Zero-shot classification: compare image embedding to class-label text embeddings

**3. Audio Processing**
Whisper (OpenAI) converts speech to text with high accuracy across languages. Pipeline:
1. Audio input (WAV/MP3/M4A)
2. Chunking for long audio (30-second windows)
3. Transcription with timestamps
4. Speaker diarisation (who said what) — separate model
5. Text processing (downstream LLM tasks)

**4. Multimodal RAG**
Extend RAG to index both text documents and images:
- Text documents: standard embedding + vector store
- Images: CLIP image embeddings + vector store with image metadata
- At query time: embed query as both text (for text search) and text (for CLIP image search)
- RRF fusion of text results + image results
- Pass retrieved images to VLM alongside retrieved text for generation

**5. Document Understanding**
Many documents combine layout, images, tables, and text (PDFs, invoices, forms):
- PDF → extract text (pdfplumber) + images (pdf2image) separately
- Use VLM for diagram/chart understanding
- Merge text + image understanding for final answer

**6. Cost & Latency Considerations**
- Image tokens: 1 image ≈ 765-2000 tokens depending on resolution and model
- Always downscale images to the minimum resolution that preserves relevant detail
- Cache processed image descriptions for repeated reference to the same image

sections.title: VLM Pipeline, Image Embedding & Multimodal RAG
Common Multimodal AI Mistakes
sections.content:
**1. Sending high-resolution images unnecessarily**
A 4K image costs 2,000+ tokens with GPT-4o Vision. For most use cases (document OCR, chart reading), 512-1024px is sufficient. Always downscale before sending.

**2. Not caching VLM image descriptions**
If the same image is referenced in multiple queries, calling the VLM each time is wasteful. Cache descriptions by image hash; invalidate on image update.

**3. Treating audio transcription as perfect**
Whisper has ~5-10% word error rate on accented or noisy audio. Apply post-processing (punctuation restoration, entity normalisation) and flag low-confidence segments.

**4. Ignoring image token cost in context windows**
Each image in the conversation history consumes hundreds of tokens. Long conversations with repeated image references rapidly exhaust context windows.

**5. No fallback for unsupported media types**
Not all VLMs support all image formats. Always validate MIME type and convert (e.g., HEIC → JPEG) before sending.

**6. Multimodal RAG without modality-specific chunking**
Text and image retrieval require different chunking strategies. Text uses sentence/paragraph chunks; images use the entire image. A single chunking strategy for both modalities will perform poorly on one.

sections.title: Build a Document Intelligence Pipeline
sections.content:
**Task:** Build a multimodal document Q&A system.

**Requirements:**
1. Accept PDF path → extract text (mock) and images (mock base64 stubs)
2. Index text chunks and image descriptions in separate vector stores
3. `query(question)` retrieves top-3 text chunks and top-2 images
4. Build multimodal prompt with retrieved text + image descriptions
5. Mock VLM generates an answer citing sources: `[text_chunk_1]`, `[image_2]`
6. Report retrieval time and estimated token cost (text + image)

**Bonus:** Add a `summarise_document(path)` function that: extracts all text, describes all images, and generates a structured summary with sections: Overview, Key Data, Charts/Figures.

sections.title: Interview Questions — Multimodal AI Systems
sections.content:
**Conceptual:**
Q: How does CLIP enable zero-shot image classification without training on the target classes?
A: CLIP trains image and text encoders in the same embedding space via contrastive learning. At inference, encode the image and each class label as text (e.g., 'a photo of a cat'). The class whose text embedding is most similar to the image embedding wins — no class-specific training needed.

**Code-based:**
Q: A user submits a 10 MB PNG image. Before sending to the VLM API, what processing should you apply?
A: (1) Resize to max 1024px on the longest dimension while preserving aspect ratio. (2) Convert to JPEG with quality=85 to reduce size 5-10×. (3) Validate the resulting file is < 20 MB (API limit). (4) Base64 encode. (5) Cache by image hash to avoid re-sending the same image.

**Senior scenario:**
Q: Your multimodal RAG system must answer questions about a 200-page technical manual with 50 diagrams. Context window is 8,000 tokens. How do you handle this?
A: (1) Offline: describe all diagrams with VLM, store descriptions + embeddings. (2) Build text chunk index (page-level) + image embedding index (diagram-level). (3) At query time: retrieve top-3 text chunks (≈300 tokens each) + top-2 diagram descriptions. (4) For retrieved diagrams, pass the actual image bytes to the VLM alongside text context. (5) Total context budget: 900 (text) + 2×765 (image tokens) + 200 (prompt) ≈ 2,630 tokens — well within 8K. (6) Apply cross-encoder re-ranking on text chunks before including.

**Wrong answer to avoid:**
Q: Why not pass all 50 diagrams to the VLM when answering a question?
Bad: 'VLMs can handle many images.' Correct: 50 images × 765+ tokens = 38,250+ tokens — exceeds most context windows and costs $0.10+ per query. Use retrieval to select the 1-3 most relevant diagrams.

questions.q: A 1024×1024 PNG image sent to GPT-4o Vision costs approximately how many tokens?
options: ~50 tokens
~200 tokens
~765 tokens
~5,000 tokens
questions.explanation: GPT-4o Vision tiles images into 512×512 patches, each costing ~170 tokens. A 1024×1024 image with 'high' detail = 4 tiles + base = ~765 tokens.

options: It generates image captions and searches by caption text
It trains image and text encoders in a shared embedding space via contrastive learning
It converts images to pixel arrays and searches by pixel similarity
It uses optical character recognition to extract searchable text from images
questions.explanation: CLIP's contrastive training aligns image and text embeddings so that cosine similarity between a text query embedding and an image embedding reflects semantic relevance.

questions.q: For Whisper audio transcription of a 2-hour meeting, you should:
options: Send the full 2-hour audio file in a single API call
Chunk audio into 30-second windows and transcribe each chunk separately
Convert to text manually before processing
Use a text model instead
questions.explanation: Whisper processes audio in 30-second windows. Long audio files must be chunked to avoid timeouts and memory limits. Chunking with slight overlap prevents clipped words at boundaries.

questions.q: In multimodal RAG, why must image retrieval use a different embedding strategy than text retrieval?
options: Images and text have different file formats
Image embeddings (CLIP-style joint vision-text space) are semantically different from text-only embeddings; searching text index with an image query produces poor results
Images cost more to embed
Vector databases don't support mixed modalities
questions.explanation: A text embedding model encodes language semantics; CLIP-style embeddings encode visual-semantic similarity. A text query must be embedded with CLIP (not text-only embedder) to find visually similar images.

questions.q: The most important optimisation when using VLMs with repeated image references is:
options: Compressing images to grayscale
Caching VLM-generated image descriptions by image hash to avoid redundant API calls
Sending images in batches
Using a smaller resolution for all queries
questions.explanation: If the same image appears in multiple queries, re-sending it to the VLM each time wastes tokens and money. Cache the description (and optionally the embedding) by image hash.
